(5) Reference to the charterparty under which NOR is tendered. (6) Cargo details (if relevant). (7) Master's signature and vessel stamp. (8) Note of any conditions (WIFPON, WICCON) if applicable. Example: 'MV [Name], [Date] [Time], [Port]. Dear Sirs, In accordance with Charter Party dated [date], I hereby tender Notice of Readiness that the vessel is in all respects ready to load [cargo]. Yours faithfully, [Master's signature].'
What is 'CQD' (Customary Quick Despatch) and how does it differ from a fixed laytime allowance?

CQD (Customary Quick Despatch) is a laytime regime where no fixed number of laytime days or hours is agreed. Instead, the charterer must load or discharge the cargo as fast as the custom of the port permits, given the port's normal working methods and facilities. Because there is no agreed allowed laytime, there is strictly no 'demurrage' or 'despatch' in the technical sense — those terms depend on comparing actual time used against a contractually fixed allowance. If the charterer takes an unreasonably long time relative to what the port custom would allow, the shipowner's remedy is a claim for damages for detention (unliquidated damages assessed on the facts), not a pre-agreed demurrage rate. CQD is now uncommon in modern fixtures — most charter parties prefer a fixed laytime or rate-based allowance precisely because CQD disputes are harder to quantify and prove, but it still appears in some older forms and certain bulk trades.
If a charter party fixes laytime and a demurrage rate but is silent on despatch, is despatch payable when the charterer finishes early?

No. Despatch and demurrage are not symmetrical as a matter of law. Demurrage compensates the shipowner for the charterer's breach of the obligation to complete cargo operations within the allowed laytime — even if the charter party fixes laytime but omits a demurrage rate, English law generally still allows the shipowner to recover unliquidated damages for detention at a reasonable market rate, because exceeding laytime is itself a breach. Despatch, by contrast, is not compensation for any breach — it is a contractual reward for finishing early, and there is no legal default or implied entitlement to it. If the charter party is silent on despatch, none is payable at all, no matter how much time the charterer saved. Despatch must be expressly agreed, almost always as a rate tied to the demurrage rate (e.g., 'despatch to be paid at half the demurrage rate, all time saved'). This asymmetry is a common trap for charterers who assume despatch is automatically the mirror image of demurrage.
What is a 'cesser clause' and how does it interact with the shipowner's lien for demurrage?

A cesser clause provides that the charterer's personal liability under the charterparty ceases once the cargo has been loaded and freight, deadfreight, and any demurrage accrued at the load port have been paid (or, in some forms, once the cargo is loaded at all). In exchange, the shipowner is left with a lien on the cargo itself as the sole remedy for sums accruing afterward — most importantly, discharge-port demurrage. Practically, this is a mixed bargain for the shipowner: the personal covenant of the charterer (who may be well-capitalized) is replaced with an in-rem lien against cargo that may have modest resale value, may already be sold on to a third-party receiver holding a bill of lading, or may be perishable/difficult to enforce against in practice. For this reason, shipowners commonly negotiate a carve-out preserving personal liability for demurrage notwithstanding the cesser clause, or insist the cargo interests provide a letter of indemnity or guarantee before agreeing to a broad cesser provision. When analyzing a demurrage claim, always check whether a cesser clause is present and, if so, whether it has been qualified by such a carve-out — an unqualified cesser clause can significantly weaken the shipowner's practical ability to collect discharge-port demurrage from the charterer directly.
